sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient presents with right shoulder pain that has been ongoing for a few weeks, initially presenting after a period of heavy work in his backyard, including lifting and physical activity.
He describes the pain as sore and tender on the outside of the right shoulder, particularly when raising his arm.
He has a history of similar issues with the right shoulder from a previous injury while playing baseball, but this current pain is more localized to the outside of the shoulder.
He denies numbness in his arm or hands.
He has been taking Advil and icing the area with some relief, but the pain persists.
He also mentions that his feet and ankles are sore, likely due to prolonged yard work.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of a previous injury to the right shoulder while playing baseball, which has resulted in occasional issues with the area.
He denies any other significant medical history.
Review of systems: The patient denies any numbness or tingling in his arm or hands.
Current medications: I prescribed Advil as needed for pain management.
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's vital signs are as follows: temperature 97.8, heart rate 62, respirations 16, blood pressure 116/84, and oxygen saturation 99%.
Physical exam: The patient has limited active and passive range of motion in the right shoulder, with tenderness at the greater tuberosity of the humerus.
There is no tenderness at the sternoclavicular or acromioclavicular joints.
The patient has a good hand grip and intact sensation to touch on the right arm.
The capillary refill is less than three seconds.
Test Results: The patient's shoulder X-ray shows no fracture.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient's symptoms are most likely due to rotator cuff tendinopathy, a condition where the tendons of the muscles that help make the shoulder muscles are injured.
Problem list: Right shoulder pain; Rotator cuff tendinopathy; Bilateral foot and ankle pain.
Plan:
I will refer the patient to physical therapy for approximately six to eight weeks to address the rotator cuff tendinopathy.
I will also order an MRI to further evaluate the condition.
In the meantime, the patient should continue taking Tylenol as needed for pain management.
If symptoms do not improve, we may consider a steroid injection into the shoulder for relief.
I recommend the patient try soaking their feet in Epsom salts to alleviate the soreness in their feet and ankles.
Follow-up: The patient will follow up with me in six to eight weeks to re-evaluate their progress with physical therapy and determine if further treatment is necessary.
